title: Inspect Unstaged Changes with git diff
summary: Read line-level differences before deciding what belongs in the next commit.

sketchBlocks.title: Inspecting Unstaged Changes with git diff
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
The `git diff` command is a powerful tool that allows you to see the changes in your working directory that have not yet been staged for commit. This command helps you understand what modifications have been made to your files, so you can decide what to include in your next commit.

Worked example:
Imagine you have a file called `index.html` in your project. You've made some changes to this file, but you're not sure what exactly has been modified. By running `git diff`, you can see a line-by-line comparison of the changes. The output will show lines that have been added, removed, or modified, helping you to review your work before staging it.

For instance, if you added a new paragraph to `index.html`, `git diff` will highlight this addition with a `+` sign at the beginning of the line. If you removed a line, it will be marked with a `-` sign. This visual representation makes it easy to spot changes and ensure that only the intended modifications are included in your commit.

sketchBlocks.title: Interpreting the Diff Output
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
When you run `git diff`, the output can seem overwhelming at first, but it's structured to help you understand the changes clearly. Each change is prefixed with a header that indicates the file being compared. The lines starting with `---` and `+++` show the old and new file versions, respectively.

The actual changes are displayed with `-` and `+` signs. Lines with a `-` indicate content that was removed, while lines with a `+` show new content. This format allows you to quickly identify what has been added or removed.

Consider a scenario where you have a file `style.css` and you've changed the color scheme. The `git diff` output will show the old color values with `-` and the new ones with `+`. This helps you verify that the changes align with your design intentions before committing.

sketchBlocks.title: Spotting Unrelated Edits
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
One of the key benefits of using `git diff` is the ability to spot unrelated changes that might have been made unintentionally. This is crucial for maintaining a clean commit history.

Imagine you're working on a feature in `app.js`, but you also made some experimental changes to `config.js` that you don't want to commit yet. By running `git diff`, you can see all the changes across your files. This allows you to selectively stage only the changes related to your feature, leaving the experimental changes for later.

This practice helps in creating focused commits that are easier to understand and review, ensuring that your commit history remains clear and meaningful.

sketchBlocks.title: Using git diff in Your Workflow
sketchBlocks.bodyMarkdown:
Incorporating `git diff` into your workflow can significantly enhance your development process. It allows you to review changes before staging them, ensuring that only the necessary modifications are included in your commits.

For example, if you're working on a collaborative project, using `git diff` before committing can help you communicate your changes more effectively to your team. It ensures that your commits are precise and only contain relevant changes, making code reviews smoother and more efficient.

By regularly using `git diff`, you develop a habit of reviewing your work, which leads to better code quality and a more organized project history.

help.hint_1: `git diff` compares the working directory with the staging area.
